prompt: Which of the 20 standard amino acids is the only one that is achiral (its alpha-carbon is NOT a stereocenter)?
explanation: Glycine's side chain is a single hydrogen, so its alpha-carbon bears two identical H substituents and is not a stereocenter; every other standard amino acid is chiral (L-configuration in proteins).

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Amino Acids and Proteins

subquestions.stem: How many DIFFERENT groups must a carbon have to be a chiral center (stereocenter)?
subquestions.explanation: A stereocenter needs four different substituents.

subquestions.stem: What is the side chain (R group) of glycine?
subquestions.explanation: Glycine's R group is just a hydrogen atom, so its alpha-carbon has two H's.

subquestions.stem: Because its alpha-carbon has two hydrogens, how many of glycine's four substituents are identical?
subquestions.explanation: Two identical H groups mean the carbon is not a stereocenter, so glycine is achiral.

prompt: What is the name of the covalent bond that joins the carboxyl group of one amino acid to the amino group of the next in a polypeptide?
explanation: A peptide bond is an amide bond formed by a condensation (dehydration) reaction between the alpha-carboxyl of one amino acid and the alpha-amino of the next, releasing water.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 3 Biological Macromolecules (Proteins)

subquestions.stem: What small molecule is released when a peptide bond forms?
subquestions.explanation: Peptide-bond formation is a condensation/dehydration reaction that releases one water molecule.

subquestions.stem: Is forming a peptide bond a hydrolysis or a condensation (dehydration) reaction?
subquestions.explanation: Bonds form by condensation; they are broken by hydrolysis (adding water).

prompt: Which amino acid has a thiol (-SH) side chain that can form disulfide bonds stabilizing higher-order protein structure?
explanation: Two cysteine thiols oxidize to form a disulfide bond (cystine), covalently cross-linking regions of a protein.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Amino Acids and Proteins

subquestions.stem: When two cysteine -SH groups are oxidized and joined, what covalent linkage forms?
subquestions.explanation: The two thiols form a disulfide (-S-S-) bond.

subquestions.stem: Which level(s) of protein structure can disulfide bonds stabilize?
subquestions.explanation: Disulfides cross-link tertiary (within a chain) and quaternary (between chains) structure.

prompt: Which amino acid's side chain bonds back to its own alpha-amino nitrogen, forming a rigid ring that disrupts alpha-helices?
explanation: Proline's cyclic (secondary amine) structure creates a kink and cannot fit an alpha-helix well, so it often breaks secondary structure or appears in turns.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Amino Acids and Proteins

subquestions.stem: Proline is a SECONDARY amine because its side chain bonds to which backbone atom?
subquestions.explanation: The ring closes onto the backbone amino nitrogen.

subquestions.stem: Which regular secondary structure does proline tend to break?
subquestions.explanation: Proline's rigid ring is poorly accommodated in an alpha-helix.

prompt: If the pH of a solution is BELOW an amino acid's isoelectric point (pI), what is the SIGN of the amino acid's net charge?
explanation: Below the pI, excess protons keep ionizable groups protonated, so the molecule carries a net positive charge; above the pI it is net negative.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Acid-base behavior of functional groups

subquestions.stem: At a pH exactly equal to the pI, what is the average net charge?
subquestions.explanation: At the pI the net charge is zero (the zwitterion predominates).

subquestions.stem: Low pH means a HIGH or LOW concentration of H+ (protons)?
subquestions.explanation: Low pH = high [H+], which protonates groups and adds positive charge.

prompt: What term names the pH at which an amino acid or protein carries NO net electric charge?
explanation: At the isoelectric point positive and negative charges balance, giving zero net charge, so the molecule will not migrate in an electric field.

source.name: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Acid-base behavior; titration of amino acids

subquestions.stem: At its pI, will a protein migrate toward the anode, the cathode, or neither in electrophoresis?
subquestions.explanation: With zero net charge it does not migrate.

subquestions.stem: A dipolar ion that carries both a + and a - charge but is net-neutral is called a ___?

prompt: The alpha-helix and beta-pleated sheet are examples of which LEVEL of protein structure, stabilized by hydrogen bonds along the backbone?
explanation: Secondary structure (alpha-helix, beta-sheet) arises from hydrogen bonding between backbone carbonyl O and amide N-H groups, independent of the side chains.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 3 Biological Macromolecules (Protein structure)

subquestions.stem: Secondary-structure hydrogen bonds form between atoms of the SIDE CHAINS or the BACKBONE?
subquestions.explanation: Backbone C=O and N-H groups hydrogen bond.

subquestions.stem: Name the two most common secondary structures.
subquestions.explanation: The alpha-helix and the beta-pleated sheet.

prompt: In Michaelis-Menten kinetics, the Michaelis constant Km equals the substrate concentration at what fraction of Vmax?
model_answer: The substrate concentration at half of Vmax (half-maximal velocity)
explanation: Km is the substrate concentration at which velocity is Vmax/2; a lower Km reflects higher apparent affinity of the enzyme for its substrate.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Enzyme Kinetics (Michaelis-Menten)

subquestions.stem: When [S] equals Km, the reaction velocity equals what expression in terms of Vmax?
Does a LOWER Km indicate higher or lower apparent affinity for substrate?

prompt: A competitive inhibitor binds the active site. Does it INCREASE, DECREASE, or leave unchanged the apparent Km?
explanation: A competitive inhibitor competes with substrate for the active site, so more substrate is needed to reach half-maximal velocity (apparent Km rises); Vmax is unchanged because saturating substrate outcompetes the inhibitor.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Enzyme Inhibition

subquestions.stem: What happens to Vmax with a purely competitive inhibitor at saturating substrate?
subquestions.explanation: Vmax is unchanged; enough substrate outcompetes the inhibitor.

subquestions.stem: Can increasing substrate concentration overcome a competitive inhibitor?
subquestions.explanation: Yes - high [S] outcompetes a competitive inhibitor.

prompt: A pure noncompetitive inhibitor binds an allosteric site (not the active site). What happens to Vmax?
explanation: A noncompetitive inhibitor lowers Vmax (it inactivates a fraction of enzyme regardless of [S]) while Km is unchanged, because it binds E and ES equally well.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Enzyme Inhibition

subquestions.stem: What happens to Km with a PURE noncompetitive inhibitor?
subquestions.explanation: Km is unchanged in pure noncompetitive inhibition.

subquestions.stem: Does a noncompetitive inhibitor bind the active site or a separate (allosteric) site?
subquestions.explanation: It binds a separate, allosteric site.

prompt: Which of the six enzyme classes catalyzes oxidation-reduction (electron-transfer) reactions - e.g., dehydrogenases and oxidases?
explanation: Oxidoreductases catalyze redox reactions. The six classes are oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, and ligases.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Enzyme Classification (EC)

subquestions.stem: Which class transfers a functional group between molecules (e.g., kinases transferring phosphate)?
subquestions.explanation: Transferases move functional groups; kinases transfer phosphate.

subquestions.stem: Which class breaks bonds using water (e.g., proteases, lipases)?

prompt: Enzymes speed reactions by lowering the activation energy. Do they change the reaction's overall delta-G or its equilibrium position? (yes/no)
model_answer: No - enzymes do not change delta-G or the equilibrium; they only lower activation energy and increase rate.
explanation: A catalyst accelerates the forward and reverse reactions equally, reaching the same equilibrium faster; it cannot alter the thermodynamics (delta-G, Keq).

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 6 Metabolism (Enzymes)

subquestions.stem: What quantity do enzymes lower to speed up a reaction?
A catalyst speeds the forward reaction; what does it do to the reverse reaction rate?
subquestions.explanation: It speeds the reverse reaction to the same extent, so equilibrium is unchanged.

prompt: Which oxygen-transport protein shows a SIGMOIDAL O2-binding curve because of cooperative binding among its four subunits?
explanation: Hemoglobin's four subunits bind O2 cooperatively, giving a sigmoidal curve; single-subunit myoglobin is hyperbolic.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 39 Respiratory System (Hemoglobin)

subquestions.stem: How many O2-binding subunits does one hemoglobin molecule have?
subquestions.explanation: Hemoglobin is a tetramer with four subunits.

subquestions.stem: Which related single-subunit protein has a hyperbolic (non-cooperative) O2 curve?
subquestions.explanation: Myoglobin has one subunit and binds O2 non-cooperatively.

prompt: What fibrous structural protein, built from a triple helix and rich in glycine and proline, is the most abundant protein in the human body?
explanation: Collagen forms a triple-helical fiber giving tensile strength to skin, bone, tendon, and connective tissue; its repeating Gly-X-Y sequence allows tight packing.

source.name: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e — Connective tissue; the extracellular matrix

subquestions.stem: Collagen's characteristic structure is a helix made of how many polypeptide chains?
subquestions.explanation: Three chains form the triple helix.

subquestions.stem: Which small amino acid appears at every third position, letting the chains pack tightly?
subquestions.explanation: Glycine's small H side chain fits the crowded helix core.

prompt: What term describes the loss of a protein's 3-D structure (by heat or extreme pH, say) WITHOUT breaking its peptide bonds?
explanation: Denaturation disrupts non-covalent interactions and higher-order structure while leaving the primary sequence (peptide bonds) intact; it often abolishes function.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 3 Biological Macromolecules (Denaturation)

subquestions.stem: Does denaturation break the peptide (amide) bonds of the PRIMARY structure?
Name one physical or chemical agent that can denature a protein.
subquestions.explanation: Heat, extreme pH, urea, or detergents can denature proteins.

prompt: What is the term for regulation in which the END PRODUCT of a metabolic pathway inhibits an enzyme earlier in that pathway?
explanation: The end product allosterically inhibits an early (often committed-step) enzyme, preventing overproduction and conserving resources.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 6 Metabolism (Feedback inhibition)

subquestions.stem: Feedback inhibitors usually bind the active site or an ALLOSTERIC site of the target enzyme?
subquestions.explanation: They typically act at an allosteric site.

subquestions.stem: Does the end product's accumulation speed up or slow down its own production?
subquestions.explanation: Accumulated product slows its own synthesis.

prompt: Proteins absorb ultraviolet light at 280 nm mainly because of which aromatic amino acid (the strongest contributor)?
explanation: Aromatic residues (Trp, Tyr, Phe) absorb near 280 nm; tryptophan contributes most, enabling protein quantitation by A280.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Amino Acids (spectroscopic properties)

subquestions.stem: Name the three aromatic standard amino acids.
Absorbance at 280 nm is commonly used to measure what property of a protein sample?

prompt: What model of enzyme-substrate binding says the active site CHANGES SHAPE to wrap around the substrate on binding (rather than being a rigid, pre-formed complement)?
explanation: Induced fit (Koshland) holds that substrate binding triggers a conformational change that optimizes catalysis, refining the older lock-and-key idea.

source.name: LibreTexts Biochemistry (Fundamentals of Biochemistry) — Enzyme catalysis (induced fit)

subquestions.stem: What older model describes the active site as a rigid, exact complement to the substrate?
The region of an enzyme where substrate binds and catalysis occurs is called the ___?

prompt: Name the two PURINE bases found in DNA.
explanation: Purines (adenine, guanine) have a double-ring structure; pyrimidines (cytosine, thymine, uracil) have a single ring.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Structure and Function

subquestions.stem: Do purines have a single-ring or a double-ring structure?
Using the mnemonic 'PURe As Gold', which two bases are the purines?
subquestions.explanation: Adenine and Guanine are the purines.

prompt: Which pyrimidine base is found in RNA but NOT in DNA?
explanation: RNA uses uracil in place of DNA's thymine; both pair with adenine. Cytosine is the pyrimidine common to both.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14-15 Nucleic acids and RNA

subquestions.stem: Which pyrimidine does DNA use that RNA replaces with uracil?
subquestions.explanation: DNA uses thymine; RNA uses uracil.

subquestions.stem: Uracil base-pairs with which purine?

prompt: In double-stranded DNA, how many hydrogen bonds form between a guanine-cytosine (G-C) base pair?
explanation: G-C pairs share three hydrogen bonds and A-T pairs share two, so GC-rich DNA is more thermally stable (higher melting temperature).

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Structure and Function

subquestions.stem: How many hydrogen bonds are in an A-T base pair?
subquestions.explanation: A-T pairs have two hydrogen bonds.

subquestions.stem: Which pairing (G-C or A-T) makes DNA more resistant to melting (higher Tm)?
subquestions.explanation: More H-bonds means G-C is more stable.

prompt: DNA replication is described by what term, because each new double helix keeps one old (parental) strand and one new strand?
explanation: Meselson and Stahl showed replication is semiconservative: each daughter duplex has one parental and one newly synthesized strand.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Replication

subquestions.stem: In semiconservative replication, how many strands of each daughter duplex are newly made?
subquestions.explanation: One new strand per daughter duplex.

subquestions.stem: Each parental strand serves as a ___ for synthesizing the new complementary strand.
subquestions.explanation: Each old strand is a template.

prompt: In which direction does DNA polymerase synthesize a new DNA strand?
model_answer: 5' to 3' (it adds nucleotides to the 3' end)
explanation: DNA polymerase can only extend a free 3'-OH end, so synthesis proceeds 5'->3'; this constraint creates the leading and lagging strands.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Replication (enzymes)

subquestions.stem: DNA polymerase adds each new nucleotide to which end of the growing strand?
Reading the template 3'->5', the new strand therefore grows in which direction?

prompt: DNA polymerase cannot start from scratch. Which enzyme synthesizes the short RNA primer it needs to begin?
explanation: Primase lays down a short RNA primer that provides a free 3'-OH for DNA polymerase to extend.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Replication (enzymes)

subquestions.stem: Is the primer that is laid down made of DNA or RNA?
What does the primer provide that DNA polymerase requires to add nucleotides?

prompt: What are the short DNA segments synthesized discontinuously on the lagging strand called?
explanation: Because polymerase only works 5'->3', the lagging strand is built as discontinuous Okazaki fragments, later joined by DNA ligase.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Replication

subquestions.stem: Are Okazaki fragments made on the leading or the lagging strand?
Which enzyme seals the nicks between adjacent Okazaki fragments?

prompt: Which enzyme unwinds and separates the two strands of the DNA double helix at the replication fork?
explanation: Helicase breaks hydrogen bonds to unwind DNA; single-strand binding proteins keep strands apart and topoisomerase relieves supercoiling ahead of the fork.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 DNA Replication (enzymes)

subquestions.stem: Which proteins coat and stabilize the separated single strands to stop them re-annealing?
Which enzyme relieves the supercoiling/tension ahead of the replication fork?

prompt: What is the three-nucleotide START codon on mRNA that initiates translation?
explanation: AUG is the start codon and codes for methionine (the initiator Met in eukaryotes).

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 15 Genes to Proteins (Translation)

subquestions.stem: Which amino acid does the start codon AUG code for?
A codon consists of how many nucleotides?

prompt: Name the three mRNA STOP (nonsense) codons that terminate translation.
explanation: These three codons signal release factors to end translation; they do not code for any amino acid.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 15 The Genetic Code

subquestions.stem: Do stop codons code for an amino acid?
Proteins that recognize stop codons and end translation are called ___?

prompt: The ANTICODON of a tRNA base-pairs with which feature of the mRNA during translation?
explanation: Each tRNA anticodon pairs antiparallel with a complementary mRNA codon, delivering the correct amino acid.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 15 Translation (tRNA)

subquestions.stem: Which molecule carries an amino acid to the ribosome and reads the codon?
Flexible pairing at the third codon position is described by the ___ hypothesis.

prompt: Name the protective structure added to the 5' end of eukaryotic pre-mRNA during processing.
explanation: Processing adds a 5' 7-methylguanosine cap and a 3' poly-A tail and removes introns by splicing.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 15 Eukaryotic RNA processing

subquestions.stem: What is added to the 3' end of eukaryotic mRNA during processing?
Removal of introns and joining of exons is called ___?

prompt: Which enzyme synthesizes RNA from a DNA template during transcription?
explanation: RNA polymerase reads the template strand 3'->5' and builds RNA 5'->3'; unlike DNA polymerase it needs no primer.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 15 Transcription

subquestions.stem: The DNA strand actually read by RNA polymerase is called the ___ strand.
Does RNA polymerase require a primer to begin, as DNA polymerase does?

prompt: What single word describes the property of the genetic code in which most amino acids are specified by MORE THAN ONE codon?
explanation: With 64 codons for 20 amino acids plus stops, most amino acids have several synonymous codons; the code is degenerate but unambiguous (each codon specifies one amino acid).

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 15 The Genetic Code

subquestions.stem: How many possible codons exist (given a 3-nucleotide, 4-base code)?
Is a single codon ever ambiguous (coding for more than one amino acid)?
subquestions.explanation: Each codon specifies only one amino acid.

prompt: What lab technique uses repeated cycles of denaturation, annealing, and extension to EXPONENTIALLY amplify a specific DNA sequence?
explanation: PCR uses a heat-stable DNA polymerase (Taq), primers, and thermal cycling to roughly double the target DNA each cycle.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 17 Biotechnology (PCR)

subquestions.stem: What heat-stable enzyme extends the primers at high temperature in PCR?
Short synthetic DNA sequences that define the region to be amplified are called ___?

prompt: Which enzyme synthesizes DNA from an RNA template (used by retroviruses and to make cDNA)?
explanation: Reverse transcriptase makes complementary DNA (cDNA) from RNA, reversing the usual DNA->RNA flow; it is central to retroviruses such as HIV.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 17 Biotechnology (cDNA)

subquestions.stem: DNA made from an mRNA template by reverse transcriptase is called ___ DNA.
Name one virus that uses reverse transcriptase.

prompt: In E. coli, the lac operon is turned ON when which molecule (a lactose isomer) binds and inactivates the repressor?
explanation: Allolactose, an inducer derived from lactose, binds the lac repressor and releases it from the operator so transcription proceeds - an inducible operon.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 16 Gene Regulation (prokaryotic operons)

subquestions.stem: When lactose/allolactose is ABSENT, is the lac operon ON or OFF?
The lac repressor binds which DNA sequence to block transcription?

prompt: Insertion or deletion of a single nucleotide in a coding region causes what type of mutation, altering ALL downstream codons?
explanation: Because codons are read as non-overlapping triplets, adding or removing one or two nucleotides shifts the reading frame and usually garbles the rest of the protein.

source.name: OpenStax Biology 2e — Ch. 14 Mutations

subquestions.stem: A single base substitution that changes one amino acid is called a ___ mutation.
A substitution that creates a premature STOP codon is called a ___ mutation.
